title: Course Description and Objectives
text:
Course Description
How environmental and safety hazards influence public health and sustainability. Multiple perspectives on water, air, and solid waste pollution, sustainability and public safety issues.
This class is focused on providing you an introduction to the relationship between the environment and health; an introduction to environmental health; and, most importantly, an opportunity for you to reflect on your own values, learning, and goals in the context of your existence and the planet that you live on.



General Education
This course meets the requirements of Area E – Lifelong Learning.



Prerequisites
None.



Course Modality
Asynchronous Online



Course Student Learning Outcomes (SLOs)

Further their own critical self-understanding and acquire the knowledge, skills, and attitudes necessary to engage and reflect in learning and self-development practices.
Develop strategies to be integrated physiological, socio-cultural, and psychological beings to promote a holistic awareness of lifelong learning throughout their lives.
Develop themselves as responsible citizens, employees and employers, family members, and members of the global society.
Actively apply and participate in developing a lifelong commitment to health for both personal well-being (such as physical, financial, emotional, intellectual, spiritual, social/interpersonal, and/or environmental aspects) and societal responsibility.
Discuss concepts of sustainability associated with natural resources and human population.
Explore people’s attitudes and motivations to change consumptive practices to preserve natural resources.
Characterize the health outcomes of exposure to environmental pollutants, including air, water, and food.
Understand the importance of safety in protecting the public and workers from various hazards at home and workplaces.
Review careers available in environmental health and safety.

title: Course Calendar
text:
Course Calendar
This course calendar provides an overview of the topic for each week. Dates indicate the Monday of the week that you should complete the assignment. Bold dates are specific due dates. Some topics and modules span multiple weeks. You will be asked to complete reading assignments, watch lectures, and complete other activities for each module.






Important




Due dates for modules and readings are available in Canvas and below. All assigned readings are available on Leganto linked to in Canvas and below, except for Air Conditioning, which is required for purchase.
Please review the dates and materials at the beginning of the class. There may be adjustments to the readings and materials as the class progresses.
Use the links below to access the materials for each module.

title: Other Course Policies
text:
Issues involving Canvas access, submissions, quizzes, or grades must be reported to me immediately. After notifying me, contact campus IT Services (ITS) and follow their procedures. Documentation from ITS may be required. Canvas problems reported after a deadline, without prior notice or documentation, will not be accommodated.



Problems with your own computer, internet connection, software, file corruption, browser issues, time zone settings, or general device failure are not grounds for extensions or exceptions. You are expected to maintain reliable access to a functioning computer and internet connection for an online course.



Technical issues must be reported as soon as they occur. Waiting until or after a deadline substantially limits what can be done. Screenshots, error messages, timestamps, or ITS documentation may be required.



This is an asynchronous online course. Managing your technology is part of managing the course. Legitimate system failures will be handled fairly when reported promptly and through the correct channel. Personal technical failures remain your responsibility.

title: Grading Policy and Assignments
text:
This course follows the standard CSUF grading scale.






Assignment
Weight




Values Exercise
20%


Exams (2)
40%


Reflection Essays (2)
40%






If you do not attempt an assignment or do not take an exam, you will receive a WU (Withdrawal Unauthorized) as your class grade. WU is calculated as an F for your GPA, but unlike an F, it indicates that you did not submit work that could be evaluated. It is critical that you attempt each assignment.
See the CSUF Catalog for complete details.




Each student will have one make-up exam opportunity for missed exams without documented justification. The make-up exam will be shorter and carry a 5% score reduction.
Essays submitted late have a 5% reduction per day, up to a maximum of 30%.
This course has no meeting time and no attendance requirement.
No extra credit assignments are available.
All assignments are completed individually.

title: Grading Policy and Assignments
text:
Discover Your Values (20%)
This class explores environmental health from multiple perspectives. Our personal values are a key part of understanding how we see, value, and interact with the environment. This assignment will ask you to spend time thinking about your own core values.

title: Grading Policy and Assignments
text:
Exams (40%)
There will be two exams, each worth 20% of your total grade. Exams are multiple choice.

title: Grading Policy and Assignments
text:
Reflection Essays (40%)
Personal Reflections on Environmental Interconnectedness
There will be two reflection essays, each worth 20% of your grade. These assignments aim to deepen your understanding of the intricate relationship between human life and the environment. You are encouraged to reflect critically on how the concepts presented in the class might influence your thinking, lifelong learning, and your personal relationship with the environment.
Grading Criteria: Each essay has detailed instructions and a rubric attached in Canvas. Please review them carefully.

Wilson, C. (2024, September 3). AI worse than humans in every way at summarising information, government trial finds. Crikey. https://www.crikey.com.au/2024/09/03/ai-worse-summarising-information-humans-government-trial/
Xu, Z., Jain, S., & Kankanhalli, M. (2024). Hallucination is Inevitable: An Innate Limitation of Large Language Models (arXiv:2401.11817). http://arxiv.org/abs/2401.11817
Zhou, L., et al. (2024). Larger and more instructable language models become less reliable. Nature, 634(8032), 61–68. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41586-024-07930-y

title: Grading Policy and Assignments
text:
Footnotes


This policy is almost entirely based on the policy by Joe McVeigh (https://eviljoemcveigh.com/) with my own additions and modifications. Shared under the same CC license.↩︎

title: Welcome
text:
Welcome to PUBH 115: Introduction to Environmental Health and Safety!
On this Welcome Page, you will find some basic information about this course, including office hours and reading materials.
This course is online and asynchronous. That means we will not meet in person or on Zoom. Your progress through the course and learning will be self-paced. There will be assignments and materials for you to engage with. Generally, I will not be posting lectures. You should review the material and, if you have particular questions on a topic, email me directly or visit me during office hours.

Course Website
This course website has all the course material and information on the course.


Canvas
You will submit assignments on Canvas. Grades will be posted on Canvas as well.
